sn47.6:1.1: This parable is expanded at Ja 168. See Bodhi’s notes in Connected Discourses for grammatical notes. | Hesiod’s Works and Days vv. 202–12, a Greek text of around 700 BCE, includes a short parable similar to the first part of the Sutta. There, when the little bird protests, the hawk insists that the powerful will always vanquish the weak. Hesiod uses this to illustrate how far humanity has fallen into violence, saying, “But you, Perses, listen to right and do not foster violence; for violence is bad for a poor man. Even the prosperous cannot easily bear its burden, but is weighed down under it when he has fallen into delusion. The better path is to go by on the other side towards justice; for Justice beats Outrage when she comes at length to the end of the race” (Evelyn-White translation).  Perhaps he would have enjoyed the Buddhist version, which shows, rather, that the wise triumph over the strong.
sn47.6:1.2: The quail (sakuṇa) is masculine in Pali, the hawk (sakuṇagghi) feminine.
sn47.6:1.4: The construction here is unusual; I adopt Bodhi’s idiomatic rendering. More literally, the hawk “wouldn’t have been able to do this to me, that is, in battle”. The sense is clear from below.
sn47.6:3.4: In the gradual training, sense restraint is a precondition for mindfulness meditation.
